system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Study 5 - All The Things You Are 2

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 5 — Study 5 - All The Things You Are 2

_Pages 150-199._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Honeysuckle Rose motif definition

> The motif starts on the 4 of Dm7 and does a scale run to the 1, but the 3rd note of the scale (2) is
> targetted by a triad from below.

**Significance (p. 150).** This defines the structural logic of the Honeysuckle Rose motif — the specific scale degree and the technique of targeting a note with a triad from below.

## Targeting scale notes with arpeggios

> You can apply this technique (targetting scale notes with an arpeggio from below) on other notes of
> a scale as well, as I demonstrate here on this C major scale:

**Significance (p. 150).** This generalizes the Honeysuckle Rose technique into a broader compositional rule applicable across any scale degree.

## Pattern 41 chord substitution

> This Cmaj7 arpeggio can be used over Cmaj7, Am7, or F#m7b5.

**Significance (p. 150).** This is a concise substitution rule establishing three distinct harmonic contexts where the same arpeggio pattern is valid.

## Pattern 42 minor 1235

> Pattern 42 circles around the b3 of Dm7 and contains a minor 1235 pattern starting on the second
> note.

**Significance (p. 150).** This defines both the structural content (minor 1235) and the characteristic motion (circling the b3) of the pattern.

## Pattern 43 major/dominant use

> Because this pattern doesn’t have a 7 or b7 in it, it can be used over major chords, as well as
> dominant chords.

**Significance (p. 150).** This gives the harmonic rule explaining why the pattern is flexible across chord qualities — the absence of a defining 7th interval.

## Harmonic minor over dominant (Phrygian dominant)

> You can play the tonic harmonic minor scale over any dominant chord.
> For example: C harmonic minor over G7
>
> When doing so, you create a 7b9, b13 sound over that chord.
>
> Another name for the harmonic minor scale played over a dominant chord is the Phrygian dominant
> scale.

**Significance (p. 150).** This is a foundational substitution rule defining how the harmonic minor scale generates a 7b9/b13 sound over dominants and introduces the Phrygian dominant concept.

## Diminished arpeggio over dominant (b9 sound)

> Diminished arpeggios are a common way to play over dominant chord and produce a 7b9 sound.
>
> Playing a diminished arpeggio from the b9, 3, 5, or b7 of a dominant chord brings out the b9
> sound.
> For example: Abdim7 over G7

**Significance (p. 150).** This defines the rule for applying diminished arpeggios over dominant chords and specifies exactly which starting chord tones to use.

## Symmetry of diminished chords

> The convenient thing about diminished chords and arpeggios is that their inversions are identical in
> shape and notes.
>
> This is because the interval structure of diminished chords is symmetrical.
>
> If you have a look at the intervals of a Cdim7 below, you will notice that the interval between each
> chord note is a minor third.

**Significance (p. 150).** This explains the theoretical reason (symmetrical minor-third stacking) that makes diminished inversions physically identical on the fretboard — a core practical insight for the guitarist.

## Pattern 47 restricted to major chords

> Pattern 47 is an extension of pattern 43, but this one can only be used on major chords because it
> contains the 7th.

**Significance (p. 150).** This states the limiting rule that distinguishes pattern 47 from pattern 43 — the presence of the major 7th restricts its harmonic application.

## Pattern 48 voice leading

> Notice the voice leading (blue circles) starting on the high E in bar 1 and going down chromatically
> to G in bar 7.

**Significance (p. 150).** This directs the student's attention to an explicit long-range chromatic voice-leading example embedded in the phrase, making it a pedagogical focal point.

## Pattern 53 motion (13 to 5)

> This simple pattern moves from the 13 to the 5.

**Significance (p. 150).** This defines the essential melodic motion of Pattern 53 over a dominant chord, establishing its characteristic interval movement.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
